# Warm / Cool Colors — Color Temperature Is Not Just Hue

**Source:** [Color Nerd](https://www.youtube.com/@ColorNerd1) (YouTube Shorts)
**Date:** 2025-06-05
**URL:** https://www.youtube.com/shorts/XmQ9O4efPrQ
**Views:** 15,696

## Description

Color temperature is not a dimension of color perception like lightness, chroma, and hue — it's a systematic shift of both hue and saturation that depends on which hue you're starting from.

## Transcript

Lightness, chroma, and hue are all dimensions of color perception. But how warm or cool a color is — its temperature — is not a dimension of color perception. So what is it?

A lot of people think of color temperature really as just hue. They're saying, "Oh, if this yellow is going this way, it's getting warmer. Or if this green is going this way, it's getting cooler." But they're just describing shifts in hue. Color temperature is not hue. It's a combination of shifts in hue and saturation that's dependent on what hue you're talking about.

If I wanted to take this palette and shift it warmer, I would slide my whole circle over this way. And you'll notice that my reds and oranges have become slightly more vibrant. My blues and turquoises have become closer to gray. Right? So, there's a change in saturation along this line. Greens have become more yellow as well as desaturated — so my lime has become sort of olive. My indigos are also going to change in hue, shift toward magenta, and become less saturated.

So, it's a systematic shift of both saturation and hue. The same thing is true if I take a neutral palette and shift it cool.

If you're an artist and you want to learn more about the science behind color temperature and how to apply it as a painter, sign up for my course with Drawing America. It starts June 21st.

## Key Concepts

- **Color temperature ≠ hue** — it's a systematic combination of hue shift AND saturation change
- **Temperature is not a perceptual dimension** like L, C, H — it's an emergent property
- **Warming a palette** (sliding the gamut circle toward warm):
  - Reds/oranges → slightly more vibrant (increased saturation)
  - Blues/turquoises → closer to gray (decreased saturation)
  - Greens → more yellow + desaturated (lime → olive)
  - Indigos → shift toward magenta + desaturated
- **Cooling** is the reverse systematic shift
- **Hue-dependent:** the effect of warming/cooling differs depending on which hue you start from — it's not a uniform rotation
- Companion to [Is Green Warm or Cool?](https://www.youtube.com/shorts/WQYLfwa2lAI) — which showed green/purple are fundamentally neither warm nor cool spectrally
